title: Leveraging Automated Unit Tests for Unsupervised Code Translation

structured.summary: This paper proposes TransCoder-ST, an unsupervised code translation framework that leverages automated unit test generation and multilingual test translation to filter pseudo-parallel data, thereby improving semantic correctness of translations. By using EvoSuite-generated tests and iterative self-training, the method significantly outperforms prior unsupervised baselines on multiple language pairs (Java, Python, C++).

strengths.point: Innovative use of automated unit tests to filter noisy pseudo-parallel data
strengths.evidence: The approach integrates EvoSuite-generated unit tests with mutation score filtering (>90%) to ensure only semantically correct translations are used for self-training, leading to substantial improvements in computational accuracy.

strengths.point: Demonstrated significant performance gains over state-of-the-art unsupervised models
strengths.evidence: TransCoder-ST achieves up to +12.6% CA@1 improvement and reduces error rates by up to 35.7% compared to TransCoder and DOBF across Java↔Python and Java↔C++ translation tasks.

strengths.point: Comprehensive evaluation on large-scale datasets and multiple language pairs
strengths.evidence: The work evaluates on over 100K Java functions with generated tests, creating parallel sets with Python and C++ and reporting detailed ablations and online/offline training comparisons.

strengths.point: Bridges software engineering tools and machine learning for code translation
strengths.evidence: Leveraging mature tools like EvoSuite for test generation and applying multilingual test translation scripts is a novel cross-disciplinary contribution enhancing unsupervised code translation.

weaknesses.point: Limited comparison to stronger or recent baselines beyond TransCoder and DOBF
weaknesses.evidence: Evaluation omits other unsupervised approaches that may leverage semantic information or symbolic execution, leaving unclear the relative advancement of TransCoder-ST.

weaknesses.point: Strong reliance on EvoSuite and high mutation score thresholds limits generality
weaknesses.evidence: Assumes availability of high-coverage unit tests (>90% mutation score), which may not hold for many languages or complex codebases; Python support relies on translated Java tests.

weaknesses.point: Lack of analysis on false negatives and test translation fidelity
weaknesses.evidence: The paper does not quantify how often correct translations are erroneously filtered out due to incomplete or flaky tests, nor does it evaluate errors introduced by the scripted multilingual test translation.

weaknesses.point: Insufficient exploration of training hyperparameters and filtering criteria
weaknesses.evidence: Effects of beam size, filtering strictness, mutation score thresholds, and cache parameters in online training are not thoroughly ablated or justified.

weaknesses.point: Evaluation metric focuses solely on unit test pass rates, ignoring code readability and style
weaknesses.evidence: No human evaluation or metrics assessing idiomatic or maintainable code are provided, limiting understanding of practical translation quality.

weaknesses.point: Reproducibility concerns due to omitted scripts and parameter details
weaknesses.evidence: Critical components like multilingual test translation scripts, cache update policies, and EvoSuite hyperparameters are not provided or detailed.

weaknesses.point: Potential bias toward simpler functions favored by EvoSuite filtering
weaknesses.evidence: Filtering may exclude complex or large functions with lower test coverage, possibly biasing the model to easier translation cases.

weaknesses.point: Limited discussion on handling semantic equivalences not captured by unit tests
weaknesses.evidence: No mechanism to identify or reconcile translations differing semantically yet passing tests, such as integer overflow behavior differences.

weaknesses.point: No user studies or real-world migration task evaluations to assess practical impact
weaknesses.evidence: Improved CA@1 scores demonstrate technical gains, but usability and developer acceptance remain untested.

questions.question: How does the method perform when EvoSuite cannot generate high-coverage tests for target languages?
questions.motivation: Understanding limitations in less mature ecosystems is critical for assessing real-world applicability and generalization.

questions.question: Can integrating supervised fine-tuning with limited parallel data further boost TransCoder-ST?
questions.motivation: Many practical scenarios have some parallel code; exploring combined approaches might yield stronger performance.

questions.question: What is the impact of false negatives in test filtering on model recall and dataset size?
questions.motivation: Quantifying trade-offs between precision and recall in filtering is important to optimize training data quality and quantity.

questions.question: How robust is the multilingual test translation process, and what errors does it introduce?
questions.motivation: Test translation errors can propagate to filtering decisions, affecting model quality; understanding this helps improve pipeline reliability.

critique_points.point_001: Limited comparison to stronger or recent baselines beyond TransCoder and DOBF. Evaluation omits other unsupervised approaches that may leverage semantic information or symbolic execution, leaving unclear the relative advancement of TransCoder-ST
critique_points.point_002: Strong reliance on EvoSuite and high mutation score thresholds limits generality. Assumes availability of high-coverage unit tests (>90% mutation score), which may not hold for many languages or complex codebases; Python support relies on translated Java tests
critique_points.point_003: Lack of analysis on false negatives and test translation fidelity. The paper does not quantify how often correct translations are erroneously filtered out due to incomplete or flaky tests, nor does it evaluate errors introduced by the scripted multilingual test translation
critique_points.point_004: Insufficient exploration of training hyperparameters and filtering criteria. Effects of beam size, filtering strictness, mutation score thresholds, and cache parameters in online training are not thoroughly ablated or justified
critique_points.point_005: Evaluation metric focuses solely on unit test pass rates, ignoring code readability and style. No human evaluation or metrics assessing idiomatic or maintainable code are provided, limiting understanding of practical translation quality
critique_points.point_006: Reproducibility concerns due to omitted scripts and parameter details. Critical components like multilingual test translation scripts, cache update policies, and EvoSuite hyperparameters are not provided or detailed
critique_points.point_007: Potential bias toward simpler functions favored by EvoSuite filtering. Filtering may exclude complex or large functions with lower test coverage, possibly biasing the model to easier translation cases
critique_points.point_008: Limited discussion on handling semantic equivalences not captured by unit tests. No mechanism to identify or reconcile translations differing semantically yet passing tests, such as integer overflow behavior differences
critique_points.point_009: No user studies or real-world migration task evaluations to assess practical impact. Improved CA@1 scores demonstrate technical gains, but usability and developer acceptance remain untested
